message.content:
<system-reminder>
The user named this session "heat-BM". This may indicate the session's focus or intent.
</system-reminder>

Mount ₣BM (rbk-12-mvp-regime-sprues). I want to debug and first-run the new
  `skirmish` mini-gauntlet suite, built this session but never executed.

  WHAT SKIRMISH IS (already built + committed, don't rebuild — verify):
  - Suite `ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH` in Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh: runs
    enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene,
    then canonical-invest -> onboarding-sequence (builds) -> tadmor, moriah,
    srjcl, pluml.
  - Entry: rbw-tS -> rbq_qualify_skirmish (Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh) ->
    tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh.
  - New fixture `canonical-invest` (Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs):
    reuses governor_mantle + retriever_invest + director_invest, OMITS
    depot_levy. It runs against a depot the operator levied by hand — skirmish
    creates NO project per run.

  OPERATOR PRECONDITION: skirmish does not levy. Before rbw-tS there must be a
  standing canonical depot — install canonical throwaway prefixes + rbw-dL under
  the active Payor, with the canonical moniker present in rbrd.env. The first
  canonical-invest case (governor_mantle) probes that moniker and will fail if
  no depot stands.

  BRACE FOR FRESH-DEPOT ISSUES: I switched Payor accounts because of a GCP
  project-quota lockout (too many projects pending deletion to levy another).
  So expect: a fresh depot levy needed under the new Payor; possible recurrence
  of the canest family-stem / global-projectId-reservation collision (the
  canest2->canest3 bump in rbtdrk_canonical.rs:70-77 was forced by exactly this
  class of problem — a deleted depot still holding the reserved projectId);
  and possible quota/pending-deletion friction during levy.

  START BY: jjx_orient ₣BM; then survey live state — rbw-dl (PayorListsDepots),
  the active Payor regime, and rbrd.env — to decide whether a fresh canonical
  depot levy is needed before attempting rbw-tS. Baseline is the ₣BM commits
  from 99a76511c onward (skirmish, tT, RCG). tT is already runtime-validated
  (59/59); skirmish is the unrun one.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Theurge builds clean; canonical-invest cases_registered + all manifest verify arms pass. Pre-existing unrelated stale test (rbtdtk_family_stem_value asserts canest2 while source const is already canest3) left untouched.

Global replace on 5 mechanical files (bujb_jurisdiction 22 class-2 case-arms/equality-tests/buc_die-values/assert-platform-args, bujb_cli 6 doc-briefs, bujp_preflight 4 case-arms, buwz_zipper 3 enroll-descriptions, burn_regime enum declaration + its '(bubep_* identifier)' description string). bubc_constants: 3 BUBC_platforms_* values flipped + prefix-tree comment rewritten from 'bub/bube/bubep_* tree' to 'BURN node-regime enum sprue family' (blind replace would have left a false tree decomposition). BUS0: {burn_platform} quoin Values: line flipped to bunne_*, and the now-stale NOTE documenting the pre-migration bubep_ spelling removed (convention section at line 1038 already declares bunne_* canonical). No class-3 identifier embeds and no class-4 derived strings exist for this enum -- pure mechanical migration. PROOF GREEN: grep bubep_ returns nothing; grep bunne_ returns full 43-site cluster across all 7 files; buw-st BukSelfTest 28/28; fast suite 107/107 (regime-validation rbrn_all_nameplates + enrollment-validation enum cases confirm declaration<->env coherence). Last migration slice of the heat; only the final regression sweep remains.

Left bare per classification: enum description prose (rbrv precedent), RBS0 mapping display text + linked-term refs (vessel-pace precedent at RBS0:414-416), bogus negative-test fixture values, and RBRN_RUNTIME docker/podman (executable-name coupling, paddock-excluded). The three node-modes deliberately share rbnne_disabled (intended family-sharing). Local proof GREEN: theurge build clean; fast suite 107/107 (regime-validation all-nameplates lockstep + invalid-entry/dns/access-mode negatives confirm declaration<->rbrn.env coherence); dockerfile-hygiene 9/9; handbook-render 15/15. Grep audit: single grep rbnne_ returns full node cluster (8 lines rbrn_regime, 5 rbjs_sentry, 4+4 Rust, 3x5 rbrn.env, 8 RBS0); RBRN_RUNTIME confirmed still bare; zero surviving bare comparisons against the three RBRN_*_MODE variables. content.text: Let me read the paddock and survey the live depot/Payor state in parallel.

content.content:
1	## Context
2	
3	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
4	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
5	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
6	`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
7	across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
8	fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
9	enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
10	work, prone to silent drift.
11	
12	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
13	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
14	uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
15	the sweep.
16	
17	The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
18	messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
19	durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
20	discovery recipe, not here.
21	
22	## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay
23	
24	"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
25	literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
26	character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
27	API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
28	textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
29	`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
30	build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.
31	
32	MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
33	an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
34	the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
35	prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
36	(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
37	inventing rationale.
38	
39	Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
40	dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
41	local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
42	(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
43	one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
44	text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
45	concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.
46	
47	## Convention — locked
48	
49	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
50	
51	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
52	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
53	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
54	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
55	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
56	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
57	             `e` = environment, etc.)
58	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
59	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
60	
61	Full target table (all in-scope enums):
62	
63	| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
64	|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
65	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
66	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
67	| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
68	| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
69	| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
70	| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |
71	
72	Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
73	egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
74	share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
75	DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
76	variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
77	whole cluster.
78	
79	## Scope decisions — resolved
80	
81	- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
82	- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
83	  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
84	  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
85	- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
86	  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
87	  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
88	- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
89	  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
90	  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
91	  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
92	  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
93	  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
94	  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
95	  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
96	- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
97	  unchanged from original framing).
98	
99	## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work
100	
101	Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
102	the candidate list and classify each hit:
103	
104	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
105	   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
106	2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
107	   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
108	   **Migrate.**
109	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
110	   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
111	   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
112	   alone.**
113	4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
114	   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
115	   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
116	   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
117	   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
118	     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
119	   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
120	   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
121	   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
122	   `-tether`.
123	
124	### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses
125	
126	- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
127	  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
128	  `:-bind`, etc.
129	- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
130	  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
131	  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
132	  stale.
133	- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
134	  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
135	  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
136	  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
137	  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).
138	
139	### Noise ranking (classification effort)
140	
141	Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
142	prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
143	`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
144	class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
145	is the heavy classification grind.
146	
147	## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate
148	
149	Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
150	(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
151	context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
152	literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
153	declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
154	remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
155	until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.
156	
157	**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
158	sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
159	graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
160	suites.
161	
162	## Discovery recipe
163	
164	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
165	  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
166	  enum missed.
167	- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
168	  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
169	- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
170	  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
171	  tables.
172	- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
173	  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
174	  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.
175	
176	## Posture
177	
178	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
179	execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
180	pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.
181	
182	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
183	`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
184	gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
185	remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
186	above.
187	
188	## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)
189	
190	₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
191	(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
192	not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
193	concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
194	wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
195	is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
196	intact.
197	
198	What remains and matters:
199	- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
200	  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
201	  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
202	  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
203	- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
204	  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
205	- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
206	  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
207	  any late bugfix churn there.
208	
209	## What done looks like
210	
211	- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
212	  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
213	- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
214	  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
215	  profiles) updated in place.
216	- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
217	  their selecting dispatch migrated.
218	- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
219	- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
220	- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
221	  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
222	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
223	
224	## Out of scope
225	
226	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
227	  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
228	- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
229	  by decision above.
230	- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
231	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.
232	
233	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
234	
235	Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:
236	
237	1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
238	   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
239	   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
240	   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
241	2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
242	   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
243	3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
244	   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
245	   graft cloud build.
246	4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
247	   heavy classification.
248	5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
249	6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.
250	
251	Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
252	not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
work, prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
the sweep.

The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
discovery recipe, not here.

## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay

"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
whole cluster.

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

file.content:
rbgp_payor_oauth_refresh() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Refresh expired OAuth credentials following RBAGS manual procedure"
  buc_doc_lines "Use this when OAuth tokens expire after 6 months or are compromised"
  buc_doc_lines "Requires downloading new OAuth JSON from Google Cloud Console"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step 'Display OAuth refresh procedure'
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "=== Manual Payor OAuth Refresh Procedure ==="
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "OAuth credentials need to be refreshed. Follow these steps:"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "1. Navigate to APIs & Services > Credentials in Payor Project"
  buc_info "   Console URL: https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials?project=${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID}"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "2. Find existing 'Recipe Bottle Payor' OAuth client"
  buc_info ""  
  buc_info "3. Download new JSON credentials:"
  buc_info "   - Click the download icon next to the OAuth client"
  buc_info "   - Or regenerate client secret if compromised"
  buc_info ""
  date +%Y%m%d > "${ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true
  local z_today_stamp=$(<"${ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE}")
  buc_info "4. Save as timestamped file:"
  buc_info "   - Example: payor-oauth-${z_today_stamp}.json"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "5. Run installation command with new JSON:"
  buc_info "   rbgp_payor_install /path/to/payor-oauth-[timestamp].json"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "This will regenerate OAuth credentials and update RBRO file."
  buc_info ""
  
  buc_success "OAuth refresh procedure displayed"
  buc_info "Note: OAuth refresh tokens expire after 6 months of non-use in testing mode"
  buc_info "Any successful payor operation resets the 6-month timer"
}

rbgp_governor_mantle() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_depot_project_id="${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}"

  buc_doc_brief "Create or replace Governor service account in a depot"
  buc_doc_lines "This operation is idempotent: existing governor-* SAs are deleted before creating a new one"
  buc_doc_lines "Targets depot derived from RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX + RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER (RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step 'Validate input parameters'
  test -n "${z_depot_project_id}" || buc_die "RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID is empty — set RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX and RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER in rbrd.env"

  buc_step 'Authenticate as Payor'
  test -n "${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID is not set"
  test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID is not set"

  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) || buc_die "Failed to authenticate as Payor via OAuth"

  buc_step 'Validate depot project exists and is active'

content.content:
test -n "${RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID not set in environment - obtain from Cloud Console Billing and set before proceeding"

  buc_step 'Validate input parameters'
  test -n "${z_oauth_json_file}" || buc_die "OAuth JSON file path required as first argument"
  test -f "${z_oauth_json_file}" || buc_die "OAuth JSON file not found: ${z_oauth_json_file}"

  buc_step 'Parse OAuth client JSON'
  local z_client_id="" z_client_secret="" z_project_id=""
  jq -r '
    (.installed.client_id // .client_id // ""),
    (.installed.client_secret // .client_secret // ""),
    (.installed.project_id // .project_id // "")
  ' "${z_oauth_json_file}" > "${ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE}" 2>/dev/null \
    || buc_die "Failed to parse OAuth JSON file"
  { read -r z_client_id
    read -r z_client_secret
    read -r z_project_id
  } < "${ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE}"
  test -n "${z_client_id}" || buc_die "OAuth JSON file missing client_id field"
  test -n "${z_client_secret}" || buc_die "OAuth JSON file missing client_secret field"
  test -n "${z_project_id}" || buc_die "OAuth JSON file missing project_id field"

  buc_step 'Check existing credentials'
  local -r z_rbro_file="${RBDC_PAYOR_RBRO_FILE}"
  if test -f "${z_rbro_file}"; then
    buc_log_args "Existing RBRO credentials will be replaced"
  fi

  local z_refresh_token=""
  buc_step 'OAuth authorization flow'
  local -r z_auth_url="${RBGC_OAUTH_AUTHORIZE_URL}?client_id=${z_client_id}&redirect_uri=urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob&scope=openid%20email%20https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform%20https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-billing&response_type=code&access_type=offline"

  buh_e
  buh_link "Open this URL in your browser: " "Google OAuth Authorization" "${z_auth_url}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "You will see four or five screens:"
  buyy_ui_yawp "Choose an account"; local -r z_yelp_choose="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "  1. ${z_yelp_choose} - Select the Google account for this payor"
  buyy_ui_yawp "Sign in to Recipe Bottle Payor"; local -r z_yelp_signin="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buyy_ui_yawp "Continue"; local -r z_yelp_continue="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "  2. ${z_yelp_signin} - Confirms account selection and previews the email-address scope; click ${z_yelp_continue}"
  buyy_ui_yawp "Google hasn't verified this app"; local -r z_yelp_unverified="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "  3. If screen says ${z_yelp_unverified}, click ${z_yelp_continue}"
  buh_line "     Otherwise, proceed to next step"
  buyy_ui_yawp "Recipe Bottle Payor wants access"; local -r z_yelp_access="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "  4. ${z_yelp_access} - Review the requested permissions"
  buh_line "     Check the permission checkboxes to grant access, then click ${z_yelp_continue}"
  buh_line "  5. Authorization code will be displayed"
  buh_e
  local z_auth_code
  z_auth_code=$(buh_prompt_secret "Copy the authorization code and paste here: ")
  test -n "${z_auth_code}" || buc_die "Authorization code is required"

  buc_log_args "Exchanging authorization code for tokens"

  # Build request and pipe to curl - secrets never touch disk
  local z_response
  z_response=$(jq -n \
    --arg code "${z_auth_code}" \
    --arg client_id "${z_client_id}" \
    --arg client_secret "${z_client_secret}" \
    --arg redirect_uri "urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob" \
    --arg grant_type "authorization_code" \
    '{
      code: $code,
      client_id: $client_id,
      client_secret: $client_secret,
      redirect_uri: $redirect_uri,
      grant_type: $grant_type
    }' | curl -sS -X POST \
      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d @- \
      "${RBGC_OAUTH_TOKEN_URL}") || buc_die "Failed to execute token exchange request"

  # Check for error in response
  local z_error
  z_error=$(jq -r '.error // empty' <<<"${z_response}")
  if test -n "${z_error}"; then
    local z_error_desc
    z_error_desc=$(jq -r '.error_description // .error // "Unknown error"' <<<"${z_response}")
    buc_info "OAuth token exchange failed (Google reported: ${z_error} - ${z_error_desc})"
    buc_info "Authorization codes are single-use and expire within minutes."
    buc_info "Re-run this command, then copy the full code and paste it promptly."
    buc_die "OAuth token exchange failed: ${z_error_desc}"
  fi

  z_refresh_token=$(jq -r '.refresh_token // empty' <<<"${z_response}")
  test -n "${z_refresh_token}" || buc_die "OAuth response missing refresh_token field"

message.content: hmm.  Why are you looking for rbro.env in downloads?  We should be using tabtargets that already know where it is.  I do think we need to mantle a governor (as payor) then create retriever and director (as governor) first.

content.text:
You're right — I went down a bad path hunting for `rbro.env`; the tabtargets own those paths and I shouldn't be guessing them. Dropping that.

But there's a wall in front of the mantle that I need to flag: **governor mantle is itself a Payor-OAuth operation.** It calls the same `zrbgp_authenticate_capture` path that `rbw-dl` just died on with `invalid_rapt`. So mantle/invest won't get off the ground until the Payor OAuth is reauthorized — and `rbw-gPR` only *prints* a procedure, it doesn't refresh anything.

The only tabtarget that actually re-mints the token is the interactive **`rbw-gPI` (PayorInstall)** flow: it opens a Google auth URL and prompts you to paste an authorization code. That's browser interaction — operator territory, not something I can drive. Its one argument is the OAuth client JSON, and the matching one is already on disk:

```
~/Downloads/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json
```

(client ID `727189059962-ddv3...` matches `RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` in your `rbrp.env`.)

So the sequence is:

1. **You** reauthorize the Payor (clears `invalid_rapt`):
   ```
   ! tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json
   ```
2. **I** then drive: governor mantle (`rbw-aM`) → retriever invest (`rbw-arI`) → director invest (`rbw-adI`).

One thing worth noting before we go manual: that mantle → retriever-invest → director-invest sequence is *exactly* what the `canonical-invest` fixture does, and it's the first non-validation block of skirmish. So once OAuth is healthy, we have a choice — run those three by hand to isolate them, or just let `rbw-tS` (skirmish) run them as its second fixture and watch where it lands.

Two questions before you reauth:

questions.question: Once OAuth is reauthorized, how do you want to run the mantle → retriever-invest → director-invest sequence?

options.label: Let skirmish run it (rbw-tS)
options.description: Run rbw-tS end-to-end; canonical-invest does the mantle+invests as its second fixture. First true first-run of skirmish; we debug wherever it stops.

options.label: Run the three by hand first
options.description: I drive rbw-aM, rbw-arI, rbw-adI individually to confirm the standing depot + SAs are healthy, then run rbw-tS afterward.

questions.question: Do we trust that the canest3bhm100001 depot actually stands under the NEW payor (rbwg-p-260521084625)? rbrd.env points at it, but rbw-dl couldn't confirm (OAuth was dead).

options.label: Verify with rbw-dl after reauth
options.description: Run rbw-dl first thing post-reauth to confirm the canonical depot exists under the new payor before any mantle attempt.

options.label: Assume it stands, proceed
options.description: Trust rbrd.env; if mantle fails on a missing depot we'll learn it then. Risks a confusing failure if the depot was levied under the old payor.

options.label: It needs a fresh levy
options.description: You know the depot does NOT exist under the new payor — we levy a fresh canonical depot (rbw-dL) before mantle.

lastPrompt: hmm.  Why are you looking for rbro.env in downloads?  We should be using tabtargets that already know where it is.  I do think we need to mantle a governor (as payor) then create retriever and directo…

content.text:
Got it — **let skirmish run the whole thing, assume the depot stands.** That's the cleanest first-run: `rbw-tS` exercises canonical-invest (mantle + both invests) as its second fixture, and we debug wherever it stops. If the `canest3bhm100001` depot turns out not to stand under the new payor, the failure will surface in canonical-invest's `governor_mantle` case — an acceptable and legible place to learn it.

Plan from here:

1. **You** reauthorize the Payor (interactive browser flow):
   ```
   ! tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json
   ```
2. **I** run `tt/rbw-tS.QualifySkirmish.sh` and read the log; we debug from wherever it lands.

A heads-up on what skirmish does *before* it ever reaches a Payor call, so we read the run correctly: it runs the four validation fixtures first (enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene), *then* canonical-invest. So the first ~75 cases should pass without touching GCP at all; the OAuth/depot reality only gets tested once it hits the mantle.

Go ahead and run the reauth — paste the auth code when prompted. Once it reports success, tell me and I'll launch skirmish.

message.content: I think payor is already authorized.  Run it now.

content.text: I'll run skirmish now.

content.text:
Found it. The sprue values are **correct** (`RBRN_ENTRY_MODE=rbnne_enabled`, `UPLINK_DNS/ACCESS_MODE=rbnne_disabled`) — so the node-mode migration is intact. The failure is unrelated to sprues: pluml's `RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=` and `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=` are **empty**, and `rbrn_validate` rejects an empty sentry hallmark.

This smells like an **ordering problem in skirmish**: `regime-validation` runs *second*, before `onboarding-sequence` (which builds/ordains and would populate hallmarks). Let me confirm two things: (1) what the all-nameplates fixture iterates over, and (2) whether the real gauntlet has the same ordering and how it survives.

message.content: I _definitely_ do not want to tolerate an empty hallmark in a validated regime.  Do you see a better option than running this case _after_ the onboarding-sequence?

content.text:
Good — the validator stays strict (`RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK 1 128` = required, min-length 1). That's correct: a validated regime is a *chargeable* regime, and that needs hallmarks. So the fix is about *ordering/scope*, not relaxing the check.

But before I answer "is reordering the best option," I want to check one thing that may sink the simple reorder: `rbrn_all_nameplates` validates **every** nameplate in `rbmm_moorings/` — and that's 5 (ccyolo, moriah, pluml, srjcl, tadmor). Skirmish only builds/charges **four** (tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml). **ccyolo is never built by skirmish.** So moving the case after onboarding-sequence still leaves ccyolo's hallmarks empty → still fails. Let me confirm against how the *gauntlet* solves the identical problem.

# Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"

content.text:
Definitive. The **gauntlet deliberately orders `regime-validation` (and regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene) *after* the build fixtures** (`pristine-lifecycle` + `onboarding-sequence`, lines 59-62). **Skirmish put them *before*** (line 82, before `canonical-invest`/`onboarding-sequence` at 85-86). That divergence is the bug — your instinct matches the gauntlet's own design exactly.

So the answer to "is there a better option than reordering": **no — reordering to match the gauntlet *is* the right fix, not a workaround.** The gauntlet already encodes the invariant that hallmark-bearing validation comes after the builds. Skirmish should mirror that.

But the ccyolo question applies to the gauntlet identically (it validates all 5, charges 4). Let me confirm how ccyolo survives — that determines whether the plain reorder is sufficient or whether there's a latent gap in *both* suites.

content.text:
Confirmed — no gap. onboarding-sequence's case list (lines 1270-1278) includes `rbtdro_onboarding_kludge_ccyolo` (line 1273), so it drives hallmarks into **all five** nameplates (tadmor + ccyolo kludged, moriah/srjcl/pluml via the ordain-conjure/airgap/bind cases). That's precisely why the gauntlet can run all-nameplates validation after onboarding-sequence and pass.

**Answer to your question: no, reordering is not a workaround — it's the correct fix, and it's what the gauntlet already does.** The gauntlet encodes the invariant "hallmark-bearing validation runs after the builds." Skirmish diverged by hoisting the three validation fixtures ahead of `canonical-invest`/`onboarding-sequence`. The fix is to mirror the gauntlet:

```
enrollment-validation     ← stays first (state-indifferent, harmless on broken state)
canonical-invest          ← skirmish's no-levy substitute for pristine + canonical-establish
onboarding-sequence       ← builds + drives hallmarks into all 5 nameplates
regime-validation         ← now sees populated hallmarks
regime-smoke
dockerfile-hygiene
tadmor / moriah / srjcl / pluml
```

Let me apply that reorder.

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${1:-}"
  local z_case="${2:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

# eof

content.text:
The reorder fix is **proven** — skirmish now flows `enrollment-validation` → `canonical-invest` exactly as the gauntlet does, with no premature validation. We're past the bug we set out to fix.

But the Payor OAuth is **still `invalid_rapt`** — `governor_mantle` died on the identical authentication wall that `rbw-dl` hit at the very start:

```
ERROR: OAuth credentials expired or invalid - run rbgp_payor_oauth_refresh: reauth related error (invalid_rapt)
```

So the payor is *not* actually authorized — the token genuinely needs reauthorization, and that's the one thing I can't do (it requires the interactive browser flow). `rbw-gPR` only prints a procedure; the command that re-mints the token is **`rbw-gPI`**, which opens a Google auth URL and prompts you to paste a code:

```
! tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json
```

`invalid_rapt` means Google is demanding a fresh reauth proof — re-running that authorization flow is what clears it. Once it reports success, I'll relaunch skirmish; it'll replay enrollment-validation + canonical-invest and we'll see whether the `canest3bhm100001` depot actually stands under the new payor (the next thing that could surface).

Two notes while you reauth:
- The skirmish reorder edit is uncommitted on disk. I'll fold it into a heat commit once we've confirmed the run gets further — no point committing a fix we haven't validated past the next checkpoint.
- If `rbw-gPI` itself errors (e.g. the auth code expired before you pasted it — they're single-use and expire in minutes), just re-run it and paste promptly.

Go ahead and run the reauth, then tell me.

lastPrompt: I _definitely_ do not want to tolerate an empty hallmark in a validated regime.  Do you see a better option than running this case _after_ the onboarding-sequence?

We are severely project lifecycle limited (quota limits) so the tweak is important.  The failure you are seeing is related to the point of that heat I believe.  Can you confirm this?

content.text: "groom BS" — that's a different heat (₣BS), which wasn't in the racing list, so it may be stabled. Let me pull it up.

content.text:
Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 66%
Heat: rbk-10-mvp-modern-organization-features (₣BS)
Status: stabled
Created: 260521


## Paces

### delete-dead-rbrp-parent-fields (₢BSAAA) [rough]

## Character
Mechanical cleanup; confirm dead-ness, then delete.

`RBRP_PARENT_TYPE` and `RBRP_PARENT_ID` are defined in `rbrp.env` and string-enrolled in `rbrp_regime.sh`, but nothing reads them — depot project creation is parentless and GCP auto-parents under the org. `RBSDE-depot_levy.adoc`'s create-project step describes a `parent:` it never actually sets — a spec/code divergence.

Done: both fields removed from `rbrp.env` and the rbrp regime validator; the RBSDE create-project step rewritten to describe the parentless create + GCP auto-parenting reality. Before deleting, `grep RBRP_PARENT` across the tree confirms zero non-definition readers.

### org-detection-from-live-parent (₢BSAAB) [rough]

## Character
Small helper; the reliable signal is the live API, never config.

The levy preflight and the override step both need to know whether the depot project is under an organization (and which one). The only trustworthy source is the project's live `parent` from the Resource Manager API — never a regime field (the just-deleted `RBRP_PARENT_TYPE` proved config diverges from reality: it read `none` while GCP auto-parented under the org).

Done: a helper the levy uses, post-create, to classify "under org (capture org id)" vs "no org" from the created project's actual parent. Consumed by the preflight and override paces that follow.

### establish-grants-payor-policy-authority (₢BSAAC) [rough]

## Character
Handbook + spec; documents a manual one-time operator step. Org-affiliated payors only.

Under a secure-by-default org, the payor needs `roles/orgpolicy.policyAdmin` to set the per-project key-creation override the levy performs. Org owners do NOT hold this by default — it must be granted deliberately. No-org payors never need it.

Done: the payor-establish handbook (`RBHPE` / `rbhpe_establish.sh`) gains a step under its existing org-affiliation (advanced) branch directing the operator to grant their identity Organization Policy Administrator on the org; `RBSPE-payor_establish.adoc` updated to match. The no-org path is untouched.

### levy-preflight-confirms-policy-authority (₢BSAAD) [rough]

## Character
Spec + impl; fail-fast guard in the levy's cheapest-first ordering. Org-affiliated payors only.

If the depot is org-affiliated, the levy must confirm the payor holds org-policy-set authority BEFORE creating any resource — otherwise it half-builds a depot it cannot finish (the exact failure hit this session: project created, governor key generation refused). Org-ness comes from the live-parent helper; the permission via `testIamPermissions` on the org.

Done: `RBSDE-depot_levy.adoc` gains a preflight step (after authenticate-as-payor, before create-project) that, for org-affiliated payors, requires the set-policy permission and otherwise dies with a clear message pointing at the establish-time grant. No-org payors skip it.

### levy-sets-project-scoped-key-override (₢BSAAE) [rough]

## Character
Spec + impl; the payoff step. The propagation wait is load-bearing. Org-affiliated payors only.

For org-affiliated depots, after the project exists the levy sets a project-scoped override clearing `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`, so governor/retriever/director key creation succeeds. Verified this session: a v1 `setOrgPolicy` `enforced:false` at project scope works, and key creation unblocks roughly a minute later. The step MUST wait out that propagation (mirroring the levy's existing IAM-propagation gate) before any SA-key-dependent step, or the subsequent governor mantle races the lag and fails.

Done: `RBSDE-depot_levy.adoc` gains the override step (org branch only) with an explicit propagation wait/verify before proceeding. No-org branch unaffected. Pairs with the establish grant and the preflight that guards it.

File-touch bitmap: (no work file changes)

content.text:
This is a sharp catch — let me confirm precisely, because there's an important distinction in *which* wall we're hitting.

First, what ₣BS is about: under a **secure-by-default organization**, the org policy `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` blocks service-account *key* creation. The payor needs `roles/orgpolicy.policyAdmin` and the levy must set a project-scoped override clearing that constraint — otherwise (per ₢BSAAD's docket) "project created, governor key generation refused." Critically, ₢BSAAB records that **config lies about org-affiliation**: `RBRP_PARENT_TYPE` read `none` while GCP auto-parented under the org. Your `rbrp.env` says `none` too — which per that pace means nothing.

Now the distinction I need to be honest about. The error I'm seeing is **not** the ₣BS failure mode, at least not yet:

- **₣BS failure**: authenticate succeeds → SA *key creation* refused by org policy (happens *deep* in the mantle).
- **Current failure**: `invalid_rapt` at the **"Authenticate as Payor"** step — the OAuth token exchange itself is rejected. It never reaches SA key creation.

So they're at different stages. **The org-policy override (₢BSAAE) will not clear `invalid_rapt`** — that's a reauth-proof-token issue, cleared only by re-running the `rbw-gPI` authorization flow.

*However* — your instinct is likely right about what's waiting one step deeper. `invalid_rapt` (reauth proof token) is frequently **enforced by an org's session-control policy** — i.e. the very secure-by-default org posture ₣BS exists to handle may be *what's demanding the reauth*. And once auth clears, the mantle's SA-key-creation step is the next thing to hit the `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` wall.

Let me verify the org-affiliation reality directly rather than trust config:

content.text:
**Confirmed — and the live evidence is striking.** Look at what gcloud just revealed:

- gcloud's active account is `bhyslop@gmail.com`, and it **cannot even see** the payor project `rbwg-p-260521084625` ("does not have permission... or it may not exist").
- But `rbrp.env` says `RBRP_OPERATOR_EMAIL=bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org`.

So the new payor lives under the **`scaleinvariant.org` Workspace identity** — an **org-affiliated** account — whereas the old payor (the one you abandoned to quota lockout) was the consumer `gmail.com` identity. The `rbtdrk_canonical.rs` comment even records the prior failure as "the prior gmail-identity payor's deleted `canest2bhm100000` depot." **You switched from a no-org consumer payor to an org-affiliated one** — and org-affiliation is the *entire reason ₣BS exists*. `RBRP_PARENT_TYPE=none` in config is exactly the lie ₢BSAAB warns about.

So yes, this failure is squarely "the point of that heat." But let me be precise about the **two distinct walls**, because they're at different stages and only one of them is what you hit *right now*:

| Stage | Wall | Cleared by |
|-------|------|-----------|
| **Authenticate as Payor** ← *we are here* | `invalid_rapt` — org session-control demands a fresh Reauth Proof Token | Re-run `rbw-gPI` (interactive OAuth re-authorization) |
| **SA key creation** (deeper in mantle) ← *the ₣BS core* | `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` org policy refuses the governor key | Project-scoped org-policy override (₢BSAAE), which needs the payor to hold `roles/orgpolicy.policyAdmin` (₢BSAAC) |

The `invalid_rapt` we're seeing is the **org's reauth demand** — itself a symptom of the secure-by-default posture, but *not* the key-creation policy. The org-policy override (₢BSAAE) won't clear it; only reauthorizing will. Then, one step deeper, the `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` wall — the literal heart of ₣BS — is what I'd expect to hit next, and that's where your "gcloud tweak to tweak permissions" comes in.

And the quota connection is real: because you're project-lifecycle-locked, you *can't* churn to a fresh project to dodge this — you have to make the org-affiliated standing depot work, which means doing the ₣BS org-policy override for real.

One caveat: gcloud is currently authed as `bhyslop@gmail.com`, the wrong identity. Any org-policy / IAM operation against `scaleinvariant.org` will need gcloud authed as `bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org` (`gcloud auth login`).

How do you want to proceed?

content.text:
The new `rbro.env` (May 21) holds exactly two values — `RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET` and `RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN` — and the client ID comes from `rbrp.env` (`727189059962-ddv3...`). So it's a refresh-token credential for **your custom payor OAuth client**, not a gcloud credential. That matters for what you're proposing, and I want to be straight with you rather than just running with it:

**`rbro.env` can't directly authenticate gcloud, for two reasons:**

1. **Wrong client.** That refresh token was minted for the payor OAuth client `727189...`, not gcloud's OAuth client. gcloud won't accept it as-is.
2. **The token is the broken thing.** The `invalid_rapt` we keep hitting *is* this exact refresh token being rejected at exchange time (`rbgp_Payor.sh:108-114` does `grant_type=refresh_token` with `RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN` → Google returns `invalid_rapt`). So it can't authenticate gcloud *or* the tabtargets right now — it needs reauthorization first.

And `invalid_rapt` here is almost certainly the **`scaleinvariant.org` org's reauthentication policy** (the "session length for Google Cloud" / reauth setting Workspace admins control). It's not expiry — Google is demanding the human re-prove identity (password/2FA). That's consistent with everything: org-affiliated identity, secure-by-default, and it's the same posture ₣BS exists to handle.

So you're **right about the identity** (the active payor is the scaleinvariant.org one), but the unblock needs **two separate interactive reauths**, both of which only you can do (browser):

- **For gcloud** (to see the project + run the org-policy tweak):
  ```
  ! gcloud auth login bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org
  ```
- **For the tabtargets** (to clear `invalid_rapt` on `rbro.env` so the mantle authenticates):
  ```
  ! tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json
  ```

My suggestion: do the `gcloud auth login` first. The moment that lands, I can run `gcloud projects describe rbwg-p-260521084625` to **confirm the org parent for real** (the thing I couldn't verify before) and read the live `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` policy — which tells us definitively whether the ₣BS key-creation wall is in front of us before we spend another skirmish run finding out. Then we tackle the `rbw-gPI` reauth + the org-policy override.

Want to run the gcloud login and let me verify the org posture?

content.text:
You're right to push back — I overstated it, and I think you're correct. Let me reconsider honestly.

It **is** possible, and there are two real mechanisms a prior session could have used to make gcloud ride on `rbro.env` without you ever running `gcloud auth login`:

1. `gcloud auth activate-refresh-token <account> <refresh-token>` — registers a refresh token (even one minted for a *custom* OAuth client) into gcloud's credential store. This would explain `bhyslop@gmail.com` showing up in `gcloud auth list` despite you never doing an interactive login.
2. Mint an access token via the same bash OAuth exchange (`grant_type=refresh_token` curl), then hand it to gcloud as a bearer token (`CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN` / `--access-token-file`). gcloud doesn't care which client minted an access token.

So my "wrong client" objection was wrong — gcloud can absolutely be driven from that credential. Let me find the actual evidence of how it was wired, rather than keep speculating.
